text: This intervention was performed by Professors Marco D'Ausano and Trista Young at the Cardiac Surgery Department in Ancona, Italy.
Our Restoranotomy technique involves removal of the sternal wires and oscillating saw, patient's arterial pressure less than 100 mmHg, opening the pleural spaces,
and the tubing for prompt CPB institution ready on the operative table.
In cardiac surgery in general, and in aortic surgery in particular, we believe excellent disposition is one important key to success.
For this reason, all was done to dissect completely through the heart and all prosthesis with the heart vessel's branches.
After systemic eparonization, the left subclavian artery branch was cannulated through the interposition of an 8 mm Dacron graft.
This allowed us to institute extracorporeal circulation and to guarantee inadequate and continuous cerebral and visceral perfusion.
In fact, the very proximal offspring of the Archvassus branch hampered aortic clamping at access to the aortic root.
Therefore, to place the aortic clamp in a more distal position, the season was made to reroute the first branch, perforcing the right brain hemisphere, on the second one, perforcing the left one.
The anastomosis was performed with the heart beating and unilateral left brain perfusion at flow rate of about 1 liter per minute.
So, the clamp was placed and the route entered.
The coronary ostea were exposed and the cardioplegic arrest was achieved by a retrograde and then antegrate infusion of Cusiol Caldoprigia.
The mental procedure was then performed.
The aortic root was extensibly cleared from the surrounding tissue and the coronary buttons were prepared and fully mobilized for the following reattachment.
This is the residual dissection of the aortic root.
The valve was accessed and the annulus sized using a pearl mount magnet sizer.
Annular U-stitches with the Teflon bridges were placed then on the aortic side.
The proximal cuff of the vassalva graft was trimmed down to 8 mm.
The prosthetic valve was positioned inside of the vassalva graft.
The annular sutures were passed through the sewing ring of the prosthesis first and then through the folded proximal edge of the vassalva graft.
Once all the sutures were passed through, the valve and the graft were parachute down onto the annulus.
The sutures were tied and cut sequentially around the sewing cuff.
This step completed the first proximal layer anastomosis.
The second hemostatic layer was performed using a 4-O polypropylene running suture, starting from the commissure between the left and right annuli and progressing clockwise circumferentially.
With the cautery, proper holes for the coronary buttons were created into the Dacron graft.
The coronary buttons were sore implanted using a 6-oh polypropylene running suture.
Finally, the graft was trimmed to an appropriate length and anastomosed to the old diagram prosthesis with a 4-0 polypropylene running suture.
After the airing, coronary reperfusion was obtained and CBP went off.
This was the final result.
The patient was extirpated 10 hours later and uneventfully discharged 7 days after.
